title: Lipstick on Polyester
timeEstimate: 20–45 minutes; wax + oil + dye combo, polyester locks oil and dye under heat
stainChemistry: Lipstick is four stains in one: pigment (iron oxides, lakes), oil (castor, lanolin), wax (beeswax, carnauba), and dye (D&C reds, especially Red 27). Each part needs different treatment, in order: wax + oil first, dye last. Polyester adds two complications — it's OLEOPHILIC (grabs the oil component aggressively) and heat-sensitive (hot water sets the oil and locks Red 27 dye into the fiber permanently, plus polyester yellows over time when heat is applied to oily / pigmented residue). Long-wear / matte / 24-hour lipsticks are harder than standard formulas because they include polymer film-formers that extend the resin / film-binder challenge. The recurring trap is rubbing — pushing pigment into the fiber surface texture makes it nearly impossible to remove. Scrape excess gently first, then work from the back.
whyThisWorks: Powder preload (cornstarch / baking soda) absorbs the oil component before any moisture can spread the stain. Generous Dawn pretreat handles the wax + oil matrix that polyester grabs onto. 91% isopropyl alcohol on a cloth lifts residual D&C dye onto a transfer pad. Cool wash plus AIR-DRY locks nothing. The honest cap on polyester: long-wear / 24-hour formulas often plateau because the polymer film-former resists home solvent.
defaultAssumption: Standard lipstick — kiss mark on a collar, smear from face touch, transfer from face mask. White / light polyester = high-confidence. Long-wear / matte / 24-hour formulas = harder; pro for valuable garments. Hot-dried = often permanent.

spottingProtocol.equipment: Plastic spoon edge or dull butter knife
spottingProtocol.instruction: Scrape excess lipstick off with a plastic spoon edge or dull butter knife — flat-edge work only, never rub. Rubbing pushes pigment into the fiber surface texture and makes the stain nearly impossible to remove.

spottingProtocol.technique: Pull bulk oil up before water
spottingProtocol.instruction: Cover the residue with cornstarch or baking soda. Press gently into the spot. 10–15 min absorb. Soft-brush off. Most surface oil and wax come off this way without water.
Generous Dawn directly on the spot. Soft toothbrush 10 min. Polyester is oleophilic — extended dwell needed for the wax + oil matrix. Cool damp-cloth rinse.
Hidden seam: dab 91% IPA, blot with white cloth. If color transfers, skip alcohol — Dawn-only finish. White / light polyester is usually fine.

spottingProtocol.agent: 91% IPA Cloth Tamp for Residual Dye
spottingProtocol.equipment: 91% IPA on white cloth, paper towels under spot
spottingProtocol.instruction: Stack paper towels UNDER the spot as a transfer pad. Apply 91% IPA to a clean white cloth — never pour. Tamp the back of the spot. Replace underneath cloth as it picks up red. 2–3 passes for residual D&C dye. Ventilate; flammable, no flame.

spottingProtocol.equipment: Washing machine, cool water, drying rack
spottingProtocol.instruction: Cool machine wash with normal detergent. SKIP fabric softener (coats fiber, blocks future cleaning). AIR-DRY only. Inspect dry — wet polyester hides residual pigment that reappears once dry. NEVER hot dryer until cleared.

steps: 1. Scrape excess gently with plastic-edge — never rub.
2. Cornstarch / baking soda preload 10–15 min, soft-brush off.
3. Generous Dawn pretreat, cool, 10 min, soft toothbrush.
4. Hidden-area IPA test on dyed polyester.
5. 91% IPA cloth tamp from back with transfer pad underneath, 2–3 passes.
6. Cool machine wash + AIR-DRY. Inspect before any heat.
products: 91% Isopropyl alcohol (hidden test on dyed polyester)
professionalProtocol.temperature: cool throughout — heat sets oil and locks D&C dye on polyester
warnings: NEVER hot water — sets oil, locks dye on polyester
NEVER hot dryer until cleared — locks dye, yellows fiber
NEVER fabric softener — coats fiber, blocks cleaning
Always scrape gently, never rub — rubbing pushes pigment into fiber
Hidden test on dyed polyester before alcohol
Polyester is oleophilic — extended Dawn dwell needed
Long-wear / 24-hour / matte formulas — often plateau, pro for valuable garments

homeSolutions.agent: Lipstick Is Four Stains in One
homeSolutions.instruction: Lipstick combines wax, oil, pigment, and dye. Each part needs different treatment, in order: wax + oil first (powder + Dawn), then dye (alcohol). Polyester adds the heat trap — hot water sets the oil and locks Red 27 dye into the fiber permanently. Two hard rules: NO hot water and NO hot dryer until cleared. The other big trap is rubbing — pushes pigment into the fiber surface texture and makes the stain almost permanent.

homeSolutions.agent: Scrape Excess Gently — Plastic Spoon Edge
homeSolutions.instruction: Use a plastic spoon edge or dull butter knife to lift any excess lipstick off the surface. Flat-edge work only, never rub. Rubbing is the #1 cause of unrescuable lipstick stains — it grinds pigment into the fiber's surface texture.

homeSolutions.agent: Cornstarch or Baking Soda — Pull the Oil Up
homeSolutions.instruction: Cover the spot with cornstarch or baking soda. Press gently. 10 to 15 minutes absorb. Soft-brush off. Most surface oil and wax come off this way without any water — water-first spreads lipstick wider.
Polyester is oleophilic — it grabs the wax + oil matrix in lipstick tight. Apply a generous amount of Dawn dish soap. Work in with a soft toothbrush for 10 minutes. Cool damp-cloth rinse.
Before alcohol on dyed polyester: dab 91% isopropyl alcohol on an inside seam, blot with a white cloth. If color transfers, skip alcohol — Dawn-only finish. White / light polyester is usually fine.

homeSolutions.agent: 91% Isopropyl Alcohol for Residual Red Dye
homeSolutions.instruction: After Dawn, you may still see a faint pink / red shadow — that's the D&C dye. Stack paper towels UNDER the spot as a transfer pad. Apply 91% isopropyl alcohol to a clean white cloth — never pour on the garment. Tamp from the BACK. Replace the underneath cloth as it picks up red. 2 to 3 passes. Open windows — alcohol is flammable, no flame.

Cool machine wash with normal detergent. SKIP fabric softener — it coats the fiber and blocks any future cleaning if needed. AIR-DRY only. Inspect dry — wet polyester sometimes hides residual pigment that reappears once dry.

steps: 1. NO hot water. NO hot dryer until cleared.
2. Scrape excess gently with plastic-edge. NEVER rub.
3. Cornstarch / baking soda preload 10–15 min, soft-brush off.
4. Generous Dawn pretreat, cool, 10 min, soft toothbrush.
5. Hidden-area IPA test on dyed polyester.
6. 91% IPA on cloth from BACK with paper towel transfer pad, 2–3 passes.
7. Cool machine wash (no softener) + AIR-DRY. Inspect dry.
products: Plastic spoon edge / dull butter knife
91% Isopropyl alcohol (hidden test on dyed polyester)
diyProtocol.whenToStop: Stop on long-wear / 24-hour / matte lipstick on dyed polyester — plateau likely, pro for valuable garments. Stop if hot water or hot dryer was already used (often permanent). Stop on specialty / performance polyester (pro). Stop after 3 home cycles without progress.
warnings: NEVER hot water — sets oil, locks D&C dye
NEVER hot dryer until cleared — locks dye, yellows polyester
NEVER fabric softener — coats fiber, blocks cleaning
Always scrape gently, NEVER rub — rubbing pushes pigment into fiber
Hidden test on dyed polyester before alcohol
Polyester is oleophilic — extended Dawn dwell needed

neverDo: Never rub — pushes pigment into fiber surface texture
Never hot water on lipstick + polyester
Never fabric softener after lipstick — coats fiber
Never alcohol on dyed performance polyester without hidden test
fiberSensitivities: Polyester is oleophilic — grabs lipstick wax + oil tightly
Polyester is heat-sensitive — hot sets oil and locks D&C dye
Long-wear / 24-hour / matte formulas: often plateau because of polymer film-former
Performance / specialty polyester: pro — coatings react unpredictably
Dyed polyester: hidden alcohol test mandatory

materialWarnings: Scrape gently, never rub — rubbing is the #1 cause of permanent lipstick stains

No hot water / no hot dryer — polyester locks D&C dye
Long-wear formulas often plateau on dyed polyester
Polyester loves oil — extended Dawn dwell needed

escalation.when: Hot water or hot dryer already used (often permanent). Long-wear / 24-hour / matte lipstick on dyed polyester (likely plateau). Performance / specialty / dry-clean-only polyester. Rubbing has already pushed pigment into the fiber surface texture. 3 home cycles plateau. Valuable garment.
escalation.whatToTell: "Lipstick on [white / dyed / athletic / specialty] polyester, formula [standard / long-wear / matte / 24-hour], age [fresh / hours / hot-dried suspected], home-treated with [scrape + cornstarch preload + Dawn cool + 91% IPA cloth tamp from back + cool wash + air-dry]. No hot, no rubbing. [Residual shadow / hot-dried suspected / long-wear plateau]."
escalation.specialistType: professional dry cleaner with solvent spotting capability for combo stains

contextQuestions: Standard lipstick or long-wear / 24-hour / matte formula?
Fresh, hours old, or hot-dried already?
Has anyone rubbed the stain trying to clean it?
Color of lipstick — red, pink, plum, nude?
deepSolveHooks.modifierNotes: Long-wear / matte / 24-hour formulas are the hard subset because of polymer film-formers — often plateau and need pro. Standard formulas respond well to the powder + Dawn + alcohol sequence. Rubbing during home attempts is the #1 cause of unrescuable lipstick stains because polyester surface texture grabs pigment particles aggressively. Red 27 D&C dye is the toughest color component — multiple alcohol passes needed.
